beats.text: In Warmup, you saw something important. Same data, but the scale of 5 graph needed fewer symbols. That's because each symbol represents MORE.

Vertical picture graph on right. Scale of 5. Key highlighted. Fewer symbols than scale of 2 graph.

beats.text: Same data, fewer symbols. That's why scale of 5 is useful when numbers get bigger. What does each symbol represent when the scale is 5?
What does each symbol represent when the scale is 5?

beats.text: Right. Each symbol represents 5 items. That's the scale.

beats.text: In a picture graph, you count by 5s using symbols: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25.

In a bar graph, you count by 5s using the numbers written along the side of the graph on the axis. Look: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30. Same skip-counting, just written on the side. Both graphs count by 5s. Where do you see the 5s on the bar graph?
Where do you see the 5s on the bar graph?

validator.description: Student placed wrong number of symbols three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: 7 symbols appear one by one in Liam row, skip-counting by 5s as each appears: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35.

beats.text: Let me show you. Liam read 35 books. Each symbol stands for 5 books. I count by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35. That's 7 times I counted 5. So I place 7 symbols. The answer is 7 symbols because 35 equals seven groups of 5.

elements.description: Vertical data table. Books Read This Month. Liam=35, Emma=20, Noah=25, Olivia=40.
Horizontal picture graph. Liam row has 7 symbols placed. Emma, Noah, Olivia rows empty. Key: Each ⭐ = 5 books.

params.description: Horizontal picture graph appears. Steps Taken data. Liam row filled with 7 symbols. Emma, Noah, Olivia rows empty. Key: each symbol = 5 steps.
Data table appears alongside graph showing Steps Taken values: Liam 35, Emma 20, Noah 25, Olivia 40.

beats.text: Finish the picture graph. Use the data table and complete the graph.
Set the lengths for all remaining categories.

validator.description: Student placed Emma 4, Noah 5, Olivia 8, all correct

beats.text: Nice. You built the whole graph. Each symbol shows 5, and you used skip-counting to figure out how many.

validator.description: Student placed incorrect number of symbols on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Check the data table and count by 5s for each category.

validator.description: Student placed incorrect number of symbols on second attempt

params.description: Data table rows highlight for Emma, Noah, and Olivia.

beats.text: Here's a hint: Emma has 20. Count by 5s until you reach 20. How many 5s is that? Do the same for Noah and Olivia.

validator.description: Student placed incorrect number of symbols three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Emma row fills with 4 symbols, Noah row with 5 symbols, Olivia row with 8 symbols.

beats.text: Let me show you. Emma has 20 steps. Count by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20. That's 4 symbols. Noah has 25: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25. That's 5 symbols. Olivia has 40: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40. That's 8 symbols. Each category gets exactly the number of symbols that matches its value when you count by the scale.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph completed. All rows filled: Liam 7 symbols, Emma 4 symbols, Noah 5 symbols, Olivia 8 symbols. Key: each symbol = 5 steps.
Data table showing Steps Taken values.

params.description: Horizontal picture graph appears. Books Read This Month data. Liam=7 symbols (35 books), Emma=4 symbols (20 books), Noah=5 symbols (25 books), Olivia=8 symbols (40 books). Key visible: each symbol = 5 books.

beats.text: Use your graph to find how many fewer books Emma read than Olivia.
How many FEWER books did Emma read compared to Olivia?

validator.description: Student selected 20, correct difference between Olivia and Emma

beats.text: Right. Olivia read 40. Emma read 20. The difference is 20.

validator.description: Student selected 15: possibly subtracted symbol count (8-4=4) then multiplied by 5 incorrectly, or confused which values to compare

params.description: Emma and Olivia rows highlight.

beats.text: Look at Emma and Olivia. Count the symbols in each row, then skip-count by 5s to find how many books each person read. What's the difference?

validator.description: Student selected 40: read Olivia's total instead of finding the difference

params.description: Emma and Olivia rows highlight.

beats.text: The question asks how many FEWER Emma read than Olivia. You need the difference between them. Find both totals, then subtract.

validator.description: Student selected 60: added Olivia and Emma's totals instead of finding the difference

params.description: Emma and Olivia rows highlight.

beats.text: The word FEWER tells us to compare, not add. Find how many books Olivia read, then how many Emma read. Subtract to find the difference.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System highlights Emma's row showing 4 symbols, counts by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20. Then highlights Olivia's row showing 8 symbols, counts by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40. Visual shows subtraction: 40 minus 20 equals 20.

beats.text: Let me show you. Emma has 4 symbols. Count by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20. That's 20 books. Olivia has 8 symbols: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40. That's 40 books. The difference between 40 and 20 is 20. Emma read 20 fewer books than Olivia.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph in reading mode. Books Read This Month data. Liam=7 symbols (35), Emma=4 symbols (20), Noah=5 symbols (25), Olivia=8 symbols (40). Key visible.

params.description: Horizontal picture graph appears. Books Read data. Liam=35 (7 symbols), Emma=20 (4 symbols), Noah=25 (5 symbols), Olivia=40 (8 symbols). Key: Each star = 5 books. Scale toggle control visible with three options: Each star = 1, Each star = 2, Each star = 5. Currently set to scale of 5.
Data table appears alongside graph showing Books Read values: Liam=35, Emma=20, Noah=25, Olivia=40.

beats.text: You built this graph with scale of 5. But what would it look like with a different scale? Try it.
Toggle the scale to 1. What happens?

validator.description: Student toggled scale from 5 to 1

params.description: Symbols multiply as scale changes to 1. Olivia: 8 symbols → 40 symbols. Liam: 7 symbols → 35 symbols. Noah: 5 symbols → 25 symbols. Emma: 4 symbols → 20 symbols. Key updates to Each star = 1 book.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph. Books Read data at scale of 1. Liam=35 (35 symbols), Emma=20 (20 symbols), Noah=25 (25 symbols), Olivia=40 (40 symbols). Key: Each star = 1 book. Scale toggle control active.
Data table showing Books Read values: Liam=35, Emma=20, Noah=25, Olivia=40.

beats.text: Olivia went from 8 symbols to 40. That's a lot of symbols. Now try scale of 2.
Toggle the scale to 2.

validator.description: Student toggled scale from 1 to 2

params.description: Symbols reduce as scale changes to 2. Olivia: 40 symbols → 20 symbols. Liam: 35 symbols → 17.5 symbols (half-symbol appears). Noah: 25 symbols → 12.5 symbols (half-symbol appears). Emma: 20 symbols → 10 symbols. Key updates to Each star = 2 books.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph. Books Read data at scale of 2. Liam=35 (17.5 symbols), Emma=20 (10 symbols), Noah=25 (12.5 symbols), Olivia=40 (20 symbols). Half-symbols visible for odd values. Key: Each star = 2 books. Scale toggle control active.
Data table showing Books Read values: Liam=35, Emma=20, Noah=25, Olivia=40.

beats.text: Fewer symbols, but still more than scale of 5. Toggle back to 5.
Toggle the scale back to 5.

validator.description: Student toggled scale from 2 to 5

params.description: Graph returns to original state. Olivia: 20 symbols → 8 symbols. Liam: 17.5 symbols → 7 symbols. Noah: 12.5 symbols → 5 symbols. Emma: 10 symbols → 4 symbols. Key updates to Each star = 5 books.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph. Books Read data at scale of 5. Liam=35 (7 symbols), Emma=20 (4 symbols), Noah=25 (5 symbols), Olivia=40 (8 symbols). Key: Each star = 5 books. Scale toggle control active.
Data table showing Books Read values: Liam=35, Emma=20, Noah=25, Olivia=40.

beats.text: Same 40 books for Olivia every time. Scale of 1: 40 symbols. Scale of 2: 20 symbols. Scale of 5: just 8.
Which scale uses the fewest symbols?

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 5, correct

beats.text: Right. Scale of 5. Bigger scale, fewer symbols, same data.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph. Books Read data at scale of 5. Liam=35 (7 symbols), Emma=20 (4 symbols), Noah=25 (5 symbols), Olivia=40 (8 symbols). Key: Each star = 5 books. Scale toggle control inactive after MC question answered.
Data table showing Books Read values: Liam=35, Emma=20, Noah=25, Olivia=40.

beats.text: All three showed the same information. The scale just changes how many symbols you need. Now let's use your graph to answer some questions.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph. Books Read data at scale of 5. Liam=35 (7 symbols), Emma=20 (4 symbols), Noah=25 (5 symbols), Olivia=40 (8 symbols). Key: Each star = 5 books.
Data table showing Books Read values: Liam=35, Emma=20, Noah=25, Olivia=40.

params.description: Data table appears. Weekend Screen Time (minutes). Saturday=35, Sunday=30, Monday=40, Tuesday=25. 4 categories displayed numerically.
Horizontal bar graph appears. Bars extend sideways from left. Axis along bottom labeled 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45. All 4 bars empty.

beats.text: This bar graph is horizontal. The bars go sideways. But look at the axis along the bottom. Same skip-counting by 5s.
What comes after 35 on this axis?

validator.description: Student selected 40, correct

beats.text: Right. 40. The axis counts by 5s: 5, 10, 15... 35, 40, 45. Same pattern, just running sideways.

validator.description: Student selected 36 — counting by 1s instead of recognizing the scale of 5

params.description: Axis numbers highlight to draw attention to the scale pattern.

beats.text: Look at the numbers on the axis. They don't go 31, 32, 33. They jump by 5s. What comes after 30? After 35?

validator.description: Student selected 45 — skipped ahead, missing the next value in the sequence

params.description: Axis numbers highlight to draw attention to the sequence.

beats.text: 45 is on the axis, but it's not right after 35. Count from 30: 30, then add 5. What do you get?

validator.description: Student selected 30 — went backward instead of forward in the sequence

params.description: Axis numbers highlight to draw attention to the direction.

beats.text: 30 comes before 35, not after. The axis counts up: 0, 5, 10, 15... What's the next jump after 35?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Axis numbers highlight in sequence: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40.

beats.text: Here's how the axis works. It counts by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35. After 35, add 5 more. That gives us 40. The answer is 40. That's how a scale of 5 works on a bar graph axis.

elements.description: Data table showing Weekend Screen Time values.
Horizontal bar graph in building mode. Axis along bottom labeled 0–45 in 5s. All bars empty.

params.description: Data table appears. Weekend Screen Time (minutes). Saturday=35, Sunday=30, Monday=40, Tuesday=25.
Empty horizontal bar graph template appears. Weekend Screen Time. Scale marked 0, 5, 10... up to 45.

beats.text: Now a bar graph, also horizontal. Read the data, set the lengths of the bars to complete the graph.
Create the bar graph. Set all four bars.

validator.description: All four bars correctly set to match data table values

beats.text: That's right. You created the bar graph independently. Horizontal or vertical, you've got this.

validator.description: Student set one or more bars incorrectly on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Look at the data table and the axis. Match each value to where it lands on the scale.

validator.description: Student set one or more bars incorrectly on second attempt

params.description: Axis numbers highlight to show the skip-counting pattern.

beats.text: Let's think about this together. The axis counts by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45. For each day, find the number in the data table, then set the bar to end at that number on the axis. Where does Monday's 40 land?

validator.description: Student set one or more bars incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates setting all four bars. Saturday bar extends to 35, Sunday bar extends to 30, Monday bar extends to 40, Tuesday bar extends to 25.

beats.text: Let me show you how this works. Saturday has 35 minutes, so I set the bar to end at 35 on the axis. Sunday has 30, so the bar ends at 30. Monday has 40, the bar ends at 40. Tuesday has 25, the bar ends at 25. The bar length shows the value. Each bar ends right on the axis number that matches the data.

elements.description: Data table showing Weekend Screen Time values.
Horizontal bar graph in building mode. Weekend Screen Time. All four bars set correctly: Saturday=35, Sunday=30, Monday=40, Tuesday=25.

params.title: Weekend Screen Time
params.description: Horizontal bar graph appears. Weekend Screen Time data. Saturday=35, Sunday=30, Monday=40, Tuesday=25.
Saturday and Sunday bars highlight.

beats.text: How many minutes of screen time on Saturday and Sunday in all?
How many minutes of screen time on Saturday and Sunday in all?

validator.description: Student selected 65, correct answer (35 + 30)

beats.text: Right. 65 minutes in all. Saturday has 35, Sunday has 30. 35 plus 30 is 65. That's how you add values from a bar graph.

validator.description: Student selected 5, the scale increment instead of the sum

params.description: Saturday and Sunday bars highlight.

beats.text: You're looking at the scale. We need the total for these two days. What value does Saturday's bar reach?

validator.description: Student selected 35, only Saturday's value

params.description: Saturday and Sunday bars pulse to draw attention to both.

beats.text: You found Saturday: 35 minutes. The question asks for Saturday AND Sunday. Find Sunday's value, then add them together.

validator.description: Student selected 70, possibly doubled 35 or added Monday's value

params.description: Saturday and Sunday bars highlight separately, one after the other.

beats.text: Let's check each bar carefully. Saturday reaches 35 on the axis. Sunday reaches 30. Count by 5s if that helps. What do you get when you add 35 and 30?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System traces Saturday bar from 0 to 35, then Sunday bar from 0 to 30, showing the values on axis.

beats.text: Let me show you. Saturday's bar ends at 35. Sunday's bar ends at 30. When we add them, 35 plus 30 equals 65. The answer is 65 minutes in all. We read each bar's height, then add those values together.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph in reading mode. Weekend Screen Time data. Saturday and Sunday bars highlighted. Saturday=35, Sunday=30, Monday=40, Tuesday=25.

params.description: Vertical bar graph appears. Scale of 5. Shows completed work from prior section.

beats.text: You made picture graphs and bar graphs, both with scale of 5. You used skip-counting to figure out symbols and bar heights. Let's see what you know.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph. Scale of 5. Shows completed work.
